beats.prompt: Quick warm-up from L1 — match each statement to what it describes.

pairs.right: Rescale the prior by the likelihood

feedback.correct: Evidence rescales the prior — and a rare hypothesis starts low. Now watch a low prior fight back.
hints: From L1: posterior ∝ prior × likelihood.
One row says how to update; one says where a rare hypothesis begins.
Evidence → rescale the prior; rare → low prior.

beats.prompt: A test is 99% accurate. You test positive for a disease that strikes 1 in 100 people. What is P(disease | positive)?

About 99% — the test is 99% accurate.note: That reads P(positive | sick) as if it were P(sick | positive). With only 1 in 100 sick, the false alarms catch up.

Around 95%.note: Still too high — the base rate hasn't entered your estimate yet.

Much lower — around 50%.note: Surprising but right — 99 true positives are matched by 99 false alarms.

hints: No wrong guess — we'll count actual people.
99% is P(+|sick), not P(sick|+).
On 10,000 people it lands at 50%.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on the base rate — then continue.

interaction.title: Base rate (the prior)
interaction.body: The base rate is how common the hypothesis is before any test — the prior. A 99% test describes P(positive | sick); Bayes flips the arrow to P(sick | positive), and the base rate is the weight it must overcome.

feedback.correct: Base rate = the prior = how common it is before testing.
hints: It's the prior, by another name.
The test gives P(+|sick); you want P(sick|+).
The base rate is the anchor the evidence must move.

beats.prompt: Drag the prevalence dial and watch the confusion grid fill — see how many are truly sick vs. false alarms.

hero.structuralReadout: Of 10,000 people: 99 true positives and 99 false alarms → half of all positives are sick.

feedback.correct: Drag prevalence: at 1%, the 9,900 healthy produce 99 false alarms — as many as all 99 true positives. PPV = 99/(99+99) = 1/2.
hints: Drag the prevalence and watch the false-positive column.
Rare per person (1%) × a huge healthy crowd (9,900) = 99 false alarms.
99 TP vs 99 FP → exactly half.

beats.prompt: Using the counts from the grid, compute P(disease | positive) = TP / (TP + FP).
beats.interviewNote: Base-rate neglect / PPV is the canonical screening-test (and ML class-imbalance) interview question: prior odds 1:99 × a likelihood ratio of 99 = 1:1, so precision is 50% no matter how 'accurate' the test sounds.

feedback.correct: 99 TP / (99 TP + 99 FP) = 1/2. Among the positives, the sick and the false alarms are equal in number.
hints: Count the people: 99 true positives, 99 false positives.
PPV = TP/(TP+FP) — not the test's accuracy; the false positives are NOT negligible.

beats.prompt: 1000 coins: one double-headed, 999 fair. You pick a coin at random and flip 10 heads in a row. What is P(double-headed | 10 heads)?

feedback.correct: 10 heads is 1024× better evidence for the trick coin — but it started 999× rarer. 1024 vs 999 → 1024/2023, barely past 50%.
hints: The trick coin was 1 in 1000; 10 fair heads have chance (1/2)^10 = 1/1024.
Odds: 1 × 2^10 vs 999 → 1024 : 999 (the 10th head is what crosses even).

beats.prompt: Same 99%/99% test — sweep the base rate and compute the posterior at each prevalence.

feedback.correct: Hold the test at 99% and move only the base rate: 1% → 1/2, 10% → 11/12, 25% → 33/34. The prior is the dial.
hints: Same 99%/99% test; only prevalence changes — recount TP vs FP at each.
At 10%: 990 TP vs 90 FP. At 25%: 2475 TP vs 75 FP.

beats.prompt: Three lenses, one answer — reveal each and see them converge on 1/2.

feedback.correct: Frequencies, odds, and formula all give 1/2 — not a coincidence of the numbers.
hints: Reveal each lens.
1:99 × 99 = 1:1 is the cleanest one.
All three say 1/2.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: a weaker test — 95% sensitive, 95% specific, same 1% disease. Compute P(disease | positive).

interaction.scenario: A weaker test: 95% sensitive, 95% specific, same 1% disease, positive result.

feedback.correct: 95 TP vs 495 FP → 95/590 = 19/118 ≈ 16%. A weaker test lowers PPV quantitatively; the base-rate trap is the same shape.
hints: 10,000 people: 100 sick → 95 TP; 9,900 healthy × 5% → 495 FP.

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to review the core principle.

feedback.correct: When a hypothesis is rare, even strong evidence can leave it improbable: posterior odds = prior odds × likelihood ratio, and a tiny prior odds cancels a huge likelihood ratio.
hints: 1-in-100 disease + 99% test → 50%, because 99 false alarms match 99 true positives.
Hold the test fixed and the base rate alone moves the answer 1/2 → 11/12 → 33/34.
Next: stack a second independent test and the odds multiply.
